text: AI Playbook and Toolkit for Public Health Departments
textPreview: AI Playbook and Toolkit for Public Health Departments

alt: AI Playbook for Public Health logo

text:
Welcome participants to the Tiered Data Use for AI lesson.
Explain that this deck is designed for staff training, leadership orientation, governance preparation, or cross-program discussion.
Frame the topic in plain language: Tiered data use classifies information by sensitivity and defines which AI environments are appropriate for public, internal, confidential, and highly restricted data.
Invite participants to think about where this topic already appears in their department's workflows, vendor tools, data systems, communications, or planning conversations.
Tiered data use classifies information by sensitivity and defines which AI environments are appropriate for public, internal, confidential, and highly restricted data.
textPreview: Tiered data use classifies information by sensitivity and defines which AI environments are appropriate for public, internal, confidentia...

text: Clear data tiers turn privacy and security rules into daily practice. Staff need to know which data can be used in which AI environments, what approvals are needed, and what tools are prohibited for sensitive information.
textPreview: Clear data tiers turn privacy and security rules into daily practice. Staff need to know which data can be used in which AI environments,...

text: Without data tiers, staff may unintentionally place confidential, protected, or sovereign data into unsuitable AI environments.
textPreview: Without data tiers, staff may unintentionally place confidential, protected, or sovereign data into unsuitable AI environments.

text:
Use this slide in the Tiered Data Use for AI training module.
Use this slide to explain the practical public health reason for learning this topic before AI tools are approved or deployed.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 2, 3, 11 and Tools 10, 15, 34.
Emphasize the core guardrail: Without data tiers, staff may unintentionally place confidential, protected, or sovereign data into unsuitable AI environments.
Ask participants how this topic could affect their own program area, staff roles, community relationships, data, or public communications.
By the end of this lesson, staff should be able to:
textPreview: By the end of this lesson, staff should be able to:

text:
• Explain Tiered Data Use for AI in plain language.
• Identify appropriate public health uses and limits.
• Describe human review, governance, equity, privacy, and monitoring expectations.
• Connect the topic to the related plays and tools.

text:
Use this slide in the Tiered Data Use for AI training module.
Walk through the objectives and connect each one to a practical implementation decision.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 2, 3, 11 and Tools 10, 15, 34.
Explain that participants do not need technical expertise to use this material. The goal is enough shared understanding to participate in governance, readiness assessment, use case review, procurement, deployment, or monitoring.

Tiered data use gives staff practical rules for which AI environments can be used with which kinds of information. Public, internal, confidential, protected, restricted, and sovereign data may require different tools, approvals, vendors, contracts, security controls, and documentation. Without tiers, staff may unintentionally place sensitive data in inappropriate AI systems.

Use this slide in the Tiered Data Use for AI training module.
Use this slide to make the topic concrete through public health workflow examples.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 2, 3, 11 and Tools 10, 15, 34.
For each example, ask: What problem is being solved? What data would be used? Who reviews the AI output? What decision remains human? What could go wrong?
What data tiers does the department use? Which AI tools are approved for each tier? What vendor, contract, logging, retention, and training restrictions are required?

Expected takeaways include: Tiered data classification table, Approved AI environment list, Data-use approval pathway.
